claims.text: Consider Minkowski spacetime in 1+1 dimensions with metric signature (+,-): ds² = dt² - dx².

An observer with constant proper acceleration a > 0 (in the +x direction) follows a worldline x^μ(τ) parameterized by proper time τ, where the 4-velocity u^μ = dx^μ/dτ satisfies u^μ u_μ = 1, a^μ = Du^μ/dτ, a^μ u_μ = 0.
The proper acceleration scalar is α = √(-a^μ a_μ) = a (constant).
Introduce the rapidity φ(τ) via dt/dτ = cosh φ, dx/dτ = sinh φ.
This automatically satisfies u^μ u_μ = 1.
Differentiating and imposing constant proper acceleration yields dφ/dτ = a.
Choosing φ(0) = 0, we get φ = aτ.
Integration gives the worldline t(τ) = (1/a)sinh(aτ), x(τ) = (1/a)cosh(aτ).
claims.notes: Initial conditions t(0)=0, x(0)=1/a assumed implicitly.

claims.text: Eliminating τ produces x² - t² = 1/a², which is a hyperbola in Minkowski coordinates (one branch with x > |t|).
Introduce Rindler coordinates (η, ξ) via the explicit transformation t = (1/a)e^{aξ} sinh(aη), x = (1/a)e^{aξ} cosh(aη), where η ∈ (-∞,+∞) and ξ ∈ (-∞,+∞).
At fixed ξ = 0, this reduces exactly to the hyperbolic worldline above with proper time τ = η.
The inverse transformation is η = (1/(2a))ln((x+t)/(x-t)), ξ = (1/(2a))ln[a²(x² - t²)].
The differentials are dt = e^{aξ}[sinh(aη) dξ + cosh(aη) dη], dx = e^{aξ}[cosh(aη) dξ + sinh(aη) dη].
Substituting into ds² = dt² - dx² gives dt² - dx² = e^{2aξ}(dη² - dξ²).
Thus (restoring transverse directions) ds² = e^{2aξ}(dη² - dξ²) - dy² - dz².
For an observer at constant ξ, dξ = dy = dz = 0, so ds² = e^{2aξ} dη².
The proper time is therefore dτ = e^{aξ} dη.
The 4-acceleration magnitude measured by this observer is a e^{-aξ}.
In particular, the observer at ξ = 0 has proper acceleration exactly a.
The Rindler horizon is the surface ξ → -∞.
In Rindler coordinates this appears as a coordinate singularity: g_{ηη} = e^{2aξ} → 0 while all curvature invariants remain zero (the spacetime is flat).
t + x = a^{-1} exp[a(η + ξ)], t - x = -a^{-1} exp[a(ξ - η)].
As ξ → -∞ at fixed finite η, both t+x → 0 and t-x → 0, i.e., the surface approaches the lightlike lines x = ±t.
These are null because ds² = 0 along dt = ±dx.
These are event horizons for the Rindler observers: signals from beyond ξ = -∞ can never reach them.
The coordinate singularity is removable by returning to Minkowski coordinates, where the geometry is regular across x = |t|.
Consider a massless scalar field φ obeying the wave equation □φ = 0.

In Minkowski coordinates the field admits a mode expansion in plane-wave basis functions that are positive-frequency with respect to Minkowski time t: φ(x) = ∫₀^∞ dω/√(4πω) [a_ω e^{-iωt+ikx} + a_ω† e^{iωt-ikx}].
The Minkowski vacuum |0_M⟩ is defined by a_ω|0_M⟩ = 0 for all ω > 0.
A uniformly accelerating observer uses proper time τ ∝ η and therefore regards modes that are positive-frequency with respect to Rindler time η as defining his particles.
claims.notes: Key physical assumption about particle definition.

claims.text: The Rindler mode functions (right wedge) satisfying the wave equation and positive-frequency condition ∂_η u_ω^R ∝ -iω u_ω^R (ω > 0) are u_ω^R(η,ξ) = (1/√(4πω)) e^{-iωη} f(ξ).
The field admits an alternative expansion φ(x) = ∫₀^∞ dω/√(4πω) [b_ω u_ω^R + b_ω† (u_ω^R)* + left-wedge terms], with associated vacuum |0_R⟩ annihilated by all b_ω.

The two complete orthonormal bases {u_ω^M} and {u_ω^R} are linearly related by a Bogoliubov transformation (key physical assumption: the field operator φ is unique and can be expanded in either basis; the transformation coefficients are determined by the Klein-Gordon inner product on a Cauchy surface).
The inverse transformation expresses the Rindler operators in terms of Minkowski ones: b_ω = ∫₀^∞ dω' (α_{ωω'}* a_{ω'} - β_{ωω'}* a_{ω'}†).
The left-wedge modes must be included to obtain a complete basis across the full spacetime; they mix with the right-wedge modes through the horizon.
The Minkowski vacuum is therefore not the Rindler vacuum.
The expectation value of the Rindler number operator in the Minkowski vacuum is ⟨N_ω⟩ ≡ ⟨0_M|b_ω† b_ω|0_M⟩ = ∫₀^∞ dω' |β_{ωω'}|².

Explicit evaluation of the overlap integrals (using the relation U ∝ e^{aη} between null coordinates and performing a contour integration or invoking the Gamma-function identity Γ(1+ix)Γ(-ix) = 2π/(e^{2πx}-1)) yields |β_{ωω'}|² = δ(ω - ω') · 1/(e^{2πω/a}-1).
claims.notes: Invoked without explicit derivation, as stated.

claims.text: The Gamma-function identity used is Γ(1+ix)Γ(-ix) = 2π/(e^{2πx}-1).
claims.notes: Should be πx/(sinh(πx)); stated identity looks incorrect.

claims.text: Consequently ⟨N_ω⟩ = 1/(e^{2πω/a} - 1).
This is precisely a Bose-Einstein thermal distribution ⟨N_ω⟩ = 1/(e^{ω/T} - 1).
Comparing 2πω/a = ω/T gives T = a/(2π) in natural units.
Restoring ℏ, c, k_B gives the Unruh temperature T = ℏa/(2πck_B).
The factor 2π originates from the periodicity in imaginary Rindler time η → η + 2πi/a that makes the Minkowski vacuum appear thermal to the accelerated observer.
The same result follows from the reduced density matrix obtained by tracing over the left wedge: ρ_R = Tr_L |0_M⟩⟨0_M| is thermal at the Unruh temperature.
All steps up to the explicit form of |β_{ωω'}|² are exact.
The overlap integral itself is a standard but technical result (invoking analytic continuation across the Rindler horizon); it is invoked here without performing the contour integration explicitly.
claims.notes: Model explicitly flags this as invoked.

claims.text: The Rindler coordinates cover the right Rindler wedge (x > |t|).
Transverse coordinates y, z are unchanged in the Rindler transformation.
The wedges ξ > -∞ and ξ < -∞ correspond to causally disconnected regions.
claims.notes: ξ < -∞ is not well-defined; means beyond the horizon.

claims.text: The final Unruh temperature is T = ℏa/(2πck_B).
